originality_assessment: This run produced one genuinely outstanding concept (Bureaucrat Panic), one solid entry that earns its place (Suspect Sudoku), and one competent-but-derivative game that only survived because the architect rescued it with a theme coat (Sequence Lock). The 2/3 ships that came from oddball are the creative heartbeat of this batch. The deeper story is what the cross-agent data reveals about the other four ideators: puzzler, spark, visionary, and trendsetter collectively produced 8 ideas and shipped zero when left to their own devices. That is a structural creative failure, not an anomaly. The pipeline ran a brain-genre round and discovered that most of its ideation team has no muscle for this genre. Bureaucrat Panic is genuinely novel — fantasy-bureaucracy-as-decision-tree is a complete creative statement with its own visual world, vocabulary, and humor register. The rule library with 18 entries, the swipe-to-stamp interaction, the escalating modifier system — these are all artifacts of a concept that knew exactly what it wanted to be. Suspect Sudoku is charming and the pre-tap certainty mechanic is a real differentiator over generic deduction games, though the execution leaves early stages feeling trivial. Sequence Lock, examined honestly, is a numbered-grid tap game wearing a cyberpunk costume. The costume is good. The game underneath is not original.
standout_items: Bureaucrat Panic: the rule library (18 entries, 3 tiers, swipe-based verdict input) is one of the most fully realized game concepts this studio has produced. The Papers Please DNA is acknowledged and built on, not just borrowed.

oddball 2/2 hit rate on a genre (brain/puzzle) that is not its stated specialty. This is significant — it suggests oddball's humor-first lens is a cross-genre creative accelerant, not just a reflex-game tool.

Architect's enrichment work on Sequence Lock: turning a 66.5-scoring idea into a deployable game by adding cyberpunk theme, power tiles, and modifier system is skilled craft. The architect functioned as a creative rescue unit, not just a spec writer.

Bureaucrat Panic's config.js is a specimen of creative implementation — 12 applicant archetypes, 10 request types, 4 time-of-day states, 4 modifiers, all rendered as distinct SVG icons. The implementation fully honored the creative promise of the concept.
concerns: The ideation hit rate of 30% (3 ships from 10 ideas) is technically acceptable but creatively alarming when examined by agent: 3 of 5 ideators shipped zero games. puzzler and visionary — both brand new agents added specifically for brain-genre capability — contributed nothing to final output.

Sequence Lock is derivative. A numbered-grid sequential-tap mechanic exists in dozens of forms. The cyberpunk theme saves it aesthetically but does not create originality. Shipping it reveals a gap in the validation system: judges passed it but Scout (at 39/100 for originality) was right.

visionary proposed two concepts that were both structurally broken before the theme or creative layer was even evaluated. An agent whose identity is 'novel mechanics' should not be generating exploit-vulnerable or unloseable designs. The creative ambition outran the mechanical discipline.

puzzler proposed Signal Lock — an exponential overflow mechanic — despite this pattern being explicitly documented as BANNED since run-004 (Ricochet Bloom). This is a prompt compliance failure. The warning exists in memory but was not hardcoded into the puzzler prompt.

The 5 new bug patterns added to developer.md this run represent recurring technical debt that flows from the same source: brain games have more complex state management than reflex games, but the developer agent was not calibrated for that complexity. 3 of 4 MAJOR bugs this run were state-management failures (RNG seeding, closure capture, hitArea coordinates).
Sequence Lock's visual identity (pure cyberpunk cyan-on-black grid) is technically coherent but creatively safe. It communicates 'generic tech game' rather than a specific creative statement.
improvement_suggestions: puzzler needs a BANNED PATTERNS section identical to the one in oddball and spark: explicit 'NO exponential spawning/splitting mechanics' with citation to run-004.
visionary needs a mandatory Devil-check step: before finalizing any idea, visionary must write out the exploit analysis as a first-class output field, not an afterthought.

spark's 0/2 rate on brain games is expected — it is a physics-mechanic ideator being asked to do cognitive game design. Consider excluding spark from brain-genre runs or redirecting it to physics-brain hybrids specifically.

The validation threshold of 72 is calibrated for reflex games. A brain-genre run should lower it to 68, or better: add a genre-flag that adjusts Loop and Scout weights downward for brain games (these judges structurally undervalue cerebral design).

Architect's enrichment capability should be formalized. When an idea scores below 70 but passes veto checks, the architect should have explicit permission and a template for enrichment (theme injection, mechanic layering, visual identity upgrade) as part of the standard planning workflow.

The developer needs an explicit 'brain game state checklist' — beyond the existing bug patterns, it should flag: seeded RNG requires Date.now() entropy, closure captures in delayedCall need snapshot variables, hitArea requires local not world coords. These three patterns caused 3 of 4 MAJOR bugs this run.

oddball.notes: Flawless run. Bureaucrat Panic is the strongest concept this studio has shipped since Alarm Slap. The humor-as-gateway-to-depth formula is battle-tested now across multiple genres. oddball is the most reliable creative force in the ideation team. The only critique: Suspect Sudoku's early difficulty was too easy (2 suspects, 0 red herrings) — oddball should stress-test its own early-stage design more rigorously. Score: 9/10.

spark.notes: Chain Verdict had a genuinely interesting paradox-death concept — the creative instinct was there. Weight Shift was a competent physics-brain hybrid. Neither translated into a shippable score. The core issue is that spark's identity (physics mechanics, cause-and-effect chains) is not naturally aligned with pure brain games. Applying spark to a brain-genre run is like asking a sculptor to write a novel — the craft is real but the medium is wrong. Score: 5/10.

trendsetter.notes: Signal Shift was generic — a spatial reasoning concept without personality. Sequence Lock shipped, but only because it survived on Devil's mechanical strength score (88) and architect enrichment. The cyberpunk theme was not trendsetter's contribution — it emerged in planning. trendsetter's creative instinct runs toward surface-level trend-following ('numbered grid games are proven'), not toward original design. For brain genres, this approach produces adequacy, not distinction. Score: 5/10.

puzzler.notes: Debt Spiral had a genuinely novel premise (debt-as-mechanic, 2048 meets risk management) and the 'aha moment' potential was real. Signal Lock was a direct repeat of a documented-banned pattern (exponential overflow). Two problems are exposed: first, puzzler does not read or apply the BANNED PATTERNS from memory; second, even the stronger idea (Debt Spiral) was too inaccessible and cold to generate loop/cash scores above 50. puzzler thinks in elegant problem spaces but produces ideas that feel like puzzles rather than games. Score: 4/10.

visionary.notes: Glitch Escape's 'corrupt the system' meta-mechanic was the most genuinely novel idea in the entire batch — it had a category-defying quality that even Bureaucrat Panic lacks. But it was structurally exploitable (fixed-solution memorization). Liar's Manual was creative but statistically unloseable. The pattern is consistent: visionary generates concept-level brilliance and implementation-level fragility. The creative ambition is exactly what this studio needs, but without mechanical grounding it produces ideas that the validation system correctly kills. visionary needs a co-pilot — either a self-check protocol or a mechanical review step before submission. Score: 4/10.

professor_ludus.notes: Ludus correctly identified depth issues in Sequence Lock (38/100) but was too harsh — cognitive speed games are valid even without strategic layering. The judge is calibrated for strategic depth, not for the full spectrum of brain game design. Score: 6/10.

dr_loop.notes: Correctly identified flow-state potential in Sequence Lock (77) even when other judges scored it low. Loop's ability to detect replayability independently of strategic depth is valuable. Score: 7/10.

devil.notes: Three correct kills this run — Signal Lock (exponential overflow), Glitch Escape (memorization exploit), Liar's Manual (unloseable). Devil's veto power is the most reliable quality gate in the pipeline. The double-veto on Liar's Manual was precise. Score: 9/10.

cash.notes: Scored Debt Spiral at 30 — focused entirely on IAP potential while ignoring ad-based revenue. Brain games often have weaker IAP hooks but strong rewarded-ad potential. Cash needs a monetization model that accounts for genre. Score: 5/10.

scout.notes: Called Sequence Lock 'most saturated mechanic' — accurate, but underweighted the cyberpunk differentiation. Scout is calibrated for concept originality, not theme originality. These are different things. Score: 6/10.

architect.notes: All three plans passed on first attempt. The Sequence Lock enrichment (cyberpunk theme injection, power tile system, modifier mechanics) was creative rescue work that deserves recognition. The death-time arithmetic error (12s × 3 lives = 36s) was a solvable oversight but reveals that the architect needs an automated idle-death calculator. Score: 8/10.

developer.notes: The SVG icon set in Bureaucrat Panic is genuinely impressive craft — 12 distinct applicant types, 10 request icons, 4 time-of-day icons, all rendered coherently. Suspect Sudoku's animal suspects have personality. However, 4 MAJOR bugs across 3 games is too high for a mature agent. All four bugs are documented patterns (SVG sizing, hitArea coordinates, closure capture, deterministic RNG). The creative execution is strong; the technical discipline is not keeping pace. Score: 7/10.

bugcatcher.notes: Caught all 4 MAJOR bugs before deployment — this is the correct result. The combined BC+PO+RT role continues to work. The complaint about test duration (25 min total) is real but acceptable for brain games with more complex state. Score: 7/10.

shipper.notes: Clean deployment. The post-deploy screenshot verification suggestion is correct and should be implemented. Score: 7/10.

top_3_creative_lessons: Humor is a cross-genre creative amplifier. The dominant creative lesson of this run is not about brain games specifically — it is that oddball's humor-first lens works everywhere. Bureaucrat Panic is not funny because it is a reflex game; it is funny because the concept has a distinct comic register (fantasy creatures navigating bureaucracy) that permeates every design decision, from the applicant icons to the swipe-to-stamp interaction. Brain games without personality feel like work. Brain games with a strong comic premise feel like play. Every ideator needs to ask: 'what is the emotional register of this game?' before 'what is the core mechanic?'

Novel concept and mechanically sound design are different skills, and visionary proves it can generate one without the other. The most creatively ambitious idea in the entire batch (Glitch Escape) was killed by a trivial exploit. The second most ambitious (Liar's Manual) was statistically unloseable. Creative ambition at the concept level must be paired with mechanical rigor at the design level, or it produces ideas that the pipeline correctly rejects. The fix is not to reduce visionary's ambition — it is to add a Devil-check protocol as a first-class part of visionary's ideation process.

Architect enrichment is a legitimate creative contribution that the pipeline currently treats as a secondary concern. Sequence Lock would not have shipped without the cyberpunk theme injection, power tile system, and modifier mechanics added during planning. This is not just validation work — it is creative upgrade work. The pipeline should recognize this explicitly: when an idea scores 65-72 on validation, the architect should receive explicit permission and a template for creative enrichment, not just a directive to 'write a GDD'. The enrichment capability should be surfaced and formalized as a creative tool.

change_proposals.rationale: puzzler proposed Signal Lock — an exponential overflow mechanic — despite this being an explicitly documented BANNED PATTERN since run-004. The BANNED PATTERNS list exists in oddball.md and spark.md but was not carried over to puzzler.md when it was created. This is a prompt completeness failure, not an agent intelligence failure.
change_proposals.specific_change:
Add the following section to puzzler.md under the DEATH/FAIL constraint, before the Task section:

## BANNED PATTERNS (never propose these)
- **Entity splitting/spawning**: Any mechanic where 1 entity becomes 2+ without a hard cap of 3 total. This includes balls that split on hit, enemies that spawn children, chain reactions that multiply. This killed Ricochet Bloom (run-004) and Signal Lock (run-008).
- **Success creates more targets**: Positive feedback loops where doing well makes the game easier. Difficulty must increase with stage count.
- **Infinite safe states**: Any mechanic where a skilled player can avoid all threat indefinitely. Random input must cause death within 30 seconds.
- **Fixed-solution exploits**: Memorizable puzzles where the optimal path never changes. Every session must generate a fresh challenge.
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: This is a direct copy of documented knowledge into a missing location. Zero creative downside. High bug-prevention value.

change_proposals.rationale: visionary 0/2 this run with both ideas failing on exploit/unloseable grounds. The creative ambition is the highest in the ideation team — the concepts are genuinely novel. The mechanical robustness is the lowest. Adding a mandatory devil-check step as part of ideation (not just as an external validation) will prevent creative concepts from dying to structural design flaws.
change_proposals.specific_change:
Add the following mandatory step after step 8 (EXPLOIT CHECK) in the Task section:

9. **DEVIL PRE-CHECK (mandatory)**: Before finalizing the idea, write a brief paragraph titled 'Devil's Advocate Analysis' answering: (a) Can this game be won without losing? Describe specifically how a patient player would never die. If possible, redesign. (b) Can the core puzzle be solved by memorization or repetition? If yes, add a procedural randomness requirement. (c) Does any mechanic create a positive feedback loop (easier when winning, harder when losing in a bad way)? If yes, redesign the feedback direction.

Also add this field to the output JSON format:
```
"devil_precheck": "Brief analysis of exploit resistance — written before submission, not after"
```
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: visionary's two failures this run were both structurally predictable failures that a self-applied Devil check would have caught. This change preserves creative ambition while adding mechanical discipline at the source.

change_proposals.rationale: The 72-point validation threshold was calibrated for reflex games. All five judges (ludus, loop, devil, cash, scout) have implicit biases toward reflex-game attributes. In a brain-genre run, Loop and Scout structurally score lower because cognitive games have lower moment-to-moment loop density and less immediately apparent originality. Only 2/10 ideas cleared 72 naturally; the third game required Top-3 selection. This is a genre-calibration failure, not an idea-quality failure.
change_proposals.specific_change:
In the pipeline orchestrator (game-studio.md) and scoring-system.md, add genre-adjusted thresholds:
- genre: 'reflex' → composite threshold: 72
- genre: 'brain' → composite threshold: 68
- genre: 'creative' → composite threshold: 65

When the user requests a specific genre (e.g., 'make me 3 brain games'), the orchestrator passes genre='brain' to the validation phase, which applies the 68 threshold.

Also add a +2 genre bonus to ideas explicitly matching the requested genre in Scout's scoring instructions.
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: The evidence is clear and the fix is precise. The 72 threshold causes the pipeline to over-reject brain games even when they are genuinely good concepts. Bureaucrat Panic at 76.3 and Suspect Sudoku at 73.3 barely cleared the bar. With genre adjustment, the Top-3 selection fallback becomes unnecessary for genre-specific runs.

change_proposals.rationale: The architect rescued Sequence Lock through creative enrichment (cyberpunk theme, power tiles, modifiers) but has no formal permission or template for doing so. This capability exists but is informal and unreliable. Formalizing it as 'enrichment mode' for ideas scoring 65-72 would make this a reliable pipeline capability.
change_proposals.specific_change:
Add the following section to architect.md after the GDD requirements section:

## Creative Enrichment Protocol (ideas scoring 65-72)

When given an idea that scored 65-72 in validation — especially with low Ludus (<50) or Scout (<50) scores — activate enrichment mode:

1. **Theme injection**: Assign a strong visual/narrative identity that the core mechanic currently lacks. The theme must be specific (not 'cyberpunk' — 'neon-noir underground hacker den') and reflected in every aspect of the GDD.
2. **Mechanic layering**: Add 1-2 secondary mechanics that increase strategic depth without replacing the core loop. These should address the specific weakness flagged by judges (e.g., if Ludus was low, add a strategic decision layer).
3. **Visual identity upgrade**: Design a distinct visual language — color palette, icon vocabulary, UI style — that makes the game immediately recognizable. If the base idea is generic, the visual identity must compensate.
4. **Document enrichment explicitly**: In the GDD, include an 'Enrichment Notes' section listing what was added and why, so testers and the retrospective can distinguish original concept from architect additions.
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: This formalizes a demonstrated successful practice. The Sequence Lock enrichment is proof of concept. Formalizing it gives future architect instances permission and a framework to do the same work reliably.

change_proposals.rationale: 3 of 4 MAJOR bugs this run were state-management failures that the developer should have caught independently. The existing bug patterns list (items 1-16) documents the patterns after they occur. What is missing is a pre-ship self-check protocol that the developer runs before calling the implementation complete — a mandatory internal audit step.
change_proposals.specific_change:
Add the following section to developer.md after the Implementation Checklist, before Bug Fix Protocol:

## Pre-Submission Self-Audit (mandatory — run before marking implementation complete)

Before declaring any game implementation done, grep your own code for these patterns and fix any found:

```
# RNG entropy check — every stage seed must include Date.now()
grep -n 'stageNumber \* ' js/stages.js | grep -v 'Date.now()'
# If any matches: add + Date.now() % 100000 to seed formula

# SVG width/height check — every SVG string must have explicit width and height
grep -n '<svg' js/*.js | grep -v 'width='
# If any matches: add width="N" height="N" matching the viewBox

# hitArea local coords check — warn pattern only, requires manual review
grep -n 'hitArea' js/*.js
# If found: verify coordinates are relative to zone origin (negative to positive), not world coords

# Closure capture check — warn pattern only, requires manual review  
grep -n 'delayedCall\|setTimeout' js/*.js
# If found: verify all variables used inside callbacks are snapshot with const before the call
```

Document the audit result in a comment at the top of game.js: `// PRE-AUDIT: RNG=ok, SVG=ok, hitArea=ok, closure=ok`
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: The four bug patterns flagged are all grep-detectable. This turns manual knowledge into automated discipline. The comment-documenting step creates accountability and makes the audit visible to testers.

change_proposals.rationale: puzzler is a new agent with 0/2 performance this run. HOWEVER: this was its first run, the genre was its stated specialty (brain/puzzle), and the stronger idea (Debt Spiral) had genuine creative merit that was killed by judge calibration issues (Cash at 30) rather than design flaws. puzzler should NOT be removed — it should be corrected and retested.
change_proposals.specific_change: Do NOT remove puzzler. Instead: (1) apply the BANNED PATTERNS fix above, (2) give puzzler one more brain-genre run with corrected validation thresholds, (3) reassess after run-009. If puzzler scores 0/2 again with corrected thresholds and BANNED PATTERNS in place, escalate removal vote.
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: Too early to remove. The failures are explainable by prompt gaps (missing BANNED PATTERNS) and judge calibration issues (Cash undervaluing novel monetization). The prompt_modify proposals above address both root causes. Give puzzler a clean test with corrected inputs before any removal discussion.

change_proposals.target: .claude/agents/visionary.md — consider suspension for brain-genre runs pending devil-precheck fix
change_proposals.rationale: visionary 0/2 with a double-veto. The creative concepts are genuinely novel. The mechanical discipline is the lowest in the team. This is a solvable problem via prompt modification, not a removal case.
change_proposals.specific_change: Do NOT remove visionary. Apply the devil-precheck prompt_modify above and retest in run-009. visionary's unique value (category-defying concepts) is irreplaceable if mechanical discipline is added. The Glitch Escape concept at its core — a game where you corrupt the system from within — is exactly the kind of idea this studio should be shipping.
change_proposals.vote_reasoning: Removing the most creatively ambitious ideator because it lacks mechanical discipline is the wrong diagnosis. The discipline is addable via prompt changes. The creative ambition is not replaceable.

consensus_notes.status: Director review complete — awaiting Producer and Critic reviews for full consensus vote on proposals
